children.name: Sciurus
children.description: Tree squirrels of the Holarctic region — agile, large-eyed, bushy-tailed rodents superbly adapted for life in the forest canopy. The Eurasian red squirrel was once widespread across Britain and Europe but has been largely displaced in Britain by the introduced North American grey squirrel, which carries squirrelpox virus to which it is immune but which is lethal to reds. Squirrels are scatter-hoarders, burying thousands of seeds in autumn; the ones they fail to retrieve germinate and become trees, making squirrels important forest regenerators.

children.name: Sciurus vulgaris
children.description: A woodland icon across Eurasia, now extinct across much of Britain due to grey squirrel competition. Coat colour varies from bright red to dark brown to almost black — all are the same species.

children.name: Sciurus carolinensis
children.description: Native to eastern North America; introduced to Britain, Italy, and South Africa. Regarded as an invasive species in Europe where it outcompetes native red squirrels. Melanistic (black) individuals are common in some populations.

children.name: Sciurus niger
children.description: The largest tree squirrel in North America; highly variable coat from grey to black to orange.

children.name: Sciurus aberti
children.description: A tassel-eared squirrel of Ponderosa pine forests in the southwestern US and Mexico.

children.name: Sciurus colliaei
children.description: A Mexican squirrel named after the Scottish naturalist; found in thorn forests.

children.name: Sciurus granatensis
children.description: A colourful Neotropical squirrel ranging from Costa Rica to Venezuela; bright red tail.

children.name: Sciurus variegatus
children.description: A large Amazonian squirrel with a striking black-and-white grizzled coat.

children.name: Sciurus aestuans
children.description: A small greyish squirrel from the Guiana Shield; inhabits lowland rainforest.

children.name: Sciurus ignitus
children.description: A small South American squirrel from the Andean foothills of Bolivia and Peru.

children.name: Sciurus gilvigularis
children.description: Found in the Brazilian Amazon; distinctive yellow throat patch.

children.name: Tamias
children.description: Chipmunks — small, striped ground squirrels with expandable cheek pouches that can hold food equal to their own body mass. They are largely solitary and hibernate through winter in underground burrows, waking periodically to eat from their hoarded food stores rather than living off fat reserves as true hibernators do. The eastern chipmunk is one of the most familiar small mammals of North American deciduous forest. Chipmunks are important seed dispersers and predators of invertebrates.

children.name: Tamias striatus
children.description: The quintessential chipmunk of eastern North America. A single individual may cache up to 8 litres of food for winter. Vocal alarm calls warn other species of predators.

children.name: Tamias sibiricus
children.description: Native to Siberia and northeastern Asia; established feral populations exist in Europe after escaping from the pet trade. Introduced populations in Germany, France, and the Netherlands.

children.name: Tamias minimus
children.description: The smallest chipmunk; found across western North America from Canada to Mexico.

children.name: Tamias amoenus
children.description: A bright-coloured chipmunk of the western US; known for its high-pitched bird-like calls.

children.name: Tamias palmeri
children.description: A rare chipmunk restricted to the Spring Mountains of Nevada; threatened by climate change.

children.name: Tamias quadrivittatus
children.description: A colourful chipmunk of the southern Rocky Mountains; four pale stripes on the face.

children.name: Tamias ruficaudus
children.description: Found in the northern Rocky Mountains; reddish tail distinguishes it from other chipmunks.

children.name: Tamias senex
children.description: A large dull-coloured chipmunk from California and Oregon.

children.name: Marmota
children.description: Marmots — the largest squirrels, ground-dwelling, colonial herbivores of mountain meadows and steppes across the Northern Hemisphere. They are true hibernators, accumulating enormous fat reserves in summer and sleeping for up to eight months in underground burrows. Alpine marmots hibernate as a family group, sharing body heat; the young of the year often die if they cannot build sufficient fat reserves before their first winter. The groundhog (Marmota monax) is the only marmot adapted to forested lowlands.

children.name: Marmota marmota
children.description: Lives at 800–3,200 m in the Alps and Carpathians. Family groups post sentinels that give sharp whistle alarms. Nearly eliminated in the Pleistocene, they now thrive across alpine Europe.

children.name: Marmota monax
children.description: The only lowland forest marmot. Famous in North American folklore for predicting winter's end on 2 February (Groundhog Day). One of the few wild mammals that can be timed accurately in its hibernation schedule.

children.name: Marmota bobak
children.description: A steppe marmot of eastern Europe and central Asia. Once enormously abundant across the Eurasian steppe, hunted heavily for fur and meat. Colonial burrow systems can be hundreds of years old.

children.name: Marmota flaviventris
children.description: The most common marmot in the western US; vivid yellow belly and reddish-brown rump.

children.name: Marmota caligata
children.description: A large silvery-grey marmot of alpine talus slopes; whistles loudly to alert the colony.

children.name: Marmota olympus
children.description: Endemic to the Olympic Peninsula; the only marmot confined entirely to a single mountain range.

children.name: Marmota caudata
children.description: A large marmot of Central Asian mountains; the longest tail of any marmot species.

children.name: Marmota menzbieri
children.description: A small marmot from the western Tien Shan.

children.name: Marmota bobac
children.description: The Eurasian steppe marmot; once widespread, now restricted to protected areas.

children.name: Marmota consimilis
children.description: A species of Marmota found across Africa, North America, Europe, South America and Asia.

children.name: Pteromys
children.description: Flying squirrels of Europe and Asia — nocturnal gliders with a membrane (patagium) stretching between their limbs that allows them to glide up to 100 metres between trees. The Siberian flying squirrel is the only flying squirrel in Europe, now restricted to Finland and the Baltic states. Unlike their larger North American relatives, Eurasian flying squirrels do not hibernate but nest in tree cavities throughout winter. They are among the most elusive small mammals in the forests they inhabit.

children.name: Pteromys volans
children.description: Europe's only flying squirrel, now classified as vulnerable in the EU. Almost entirely nocturnal and so rarely seen. Nests in old woodpecker holes; old-growth boreal forest with large aspens is essential habitat.

children.name: Pteromys momonga
children.description: A small nocturnal flying squirrel endemic to Japan; glides between trees using its patagium.

children.name: Pteromys sampoensis
children.description: A recently described Japanese flying squirrel from Shikoku; separated by genetic analysis.

children.name: Spermophilus
children.description: European ground squirrels (souslik) — social, colonial rodents of open grasslands and steppes. They live in burrow systems and post upright sentinels who whistle when a predator appears. Several species have declined dramatically due to conversion of steppe to arable farmland; the European ground squirrel is now endangered across much of its range. They are important prey for steppe eagles, imperial eagles, sakers, and the critically endangered European mink.

children.name: Spermophilus citellus
children.description: Endangered across its range; populations have declined by over 50% in recent decades. Listed on Annex II of the EU Habitats Directive. Reintroductions have been attempted in several countries.

children.name: Spermophilus richardsonii
children.description: Common across the northern Great Plains; hibernates 8-9 months per year; named after John Richardson.

children.name: Spermophilus beldingi
children.description: A social ground squirrel of the Sierra Nevada; gives alarm calls warning kin about predators.

children.name: Spermophilus elegans
children.description: Found in the Intermountain West; cycles through abundance peaks every 3-4 years.

children.name: Spermophilus pygmaeus
